elements.docCommentExcerpt:
Sheaf finds the gaps between your contracts (APIs, CLIs), tests, docs,
and examples. The top-level binary dispatches to scan / doctor / serve /
report / gaps / coverage / review / init / version subcommands.

Focused inverse of `sheaf report`. Runs the full pipeline, then
emits the CoverageProfile for the single element named by --element
as either pretty-printed text or JSON.
Path to sheaf.textproto.
ContractElement ID (required).
Output format — text or json.
Project repo root.

Validates sheaf.textproto and categorization-rules.textproto, walks
every configured adapter, and probes the LLM embedder. Use before the
first scan on a new project, or whenever a scan starts producing
surprising output.
Path to sheaf.textproto (default <repo>/sheaf.textproto).
Path to the project repo root.

Runs the same pipeline as `sheaf scan` and prints the resulting
Finding list, filtered client-side. Output as text, json, or csv.
Path to sheaf.textproto.
Output format — text, json, or csv.
Filter by finding kind (MISSING_IN_CATEGORY, THIN_REFERENCE, …).
Filter by library / element-ID prefix.
Project repo root.
Minimum severity (INFO|WARNING|ERROR).

Writes a starter pair of config files (sheaf.textproto and
categorization-rules.textproto) into --repo. Refuses to overwrite
an existing sheaf.textproto. Three templates ship: minimal,
argh-cli, and fuchsia-internal.
Where to write the config files.
Template name — minimal, argh-cli, fuchsia-internal.

Loads a previously-saved Snapshot JSON (the shape `sheaf snapshot` and the
MCP server's library_snapshot op emit) and renders the canonical
self-contained HTML report from it, entirely in-process — no server connect,
no rescan. It is the in-binary replacement for `scanner --from-snapshot`,
sharing the exact same core so the two paths cannot diverge.

When --repo-root points at a real git working tree, the report's Lag section
is computed and rendered — the headline reason to render a snapshot against a
checkout rather than the bare snapshot alone.
Target API level for removed-in-the-past detection. Numeric (e.g. 27), HEAD (dev tip), or NEXT.
Optional short git hash of the scanned repo, rendered in the minimal/hero header strip.
Relative href to this library's Concept Docs report; when set, the coverage report's concept-doc reach line links to it.
Ecosystem id (rendering shape) — fidl | cli | proto | cpp | openapi | ...
Path to a Snapshot JSON (e.g. from `sheaf snapshot --out`). Rendered directly, no server. Required.
Masthead layout — full (5-KPI numtable), hero (single % bridged tile), or minimal (one-line strip, no tiles).
Library label for the report. Defaults to the snapshot's own library field.
For design iteration only — replace the Overlap (UpSet) row data with a richer fictional set.
Output HTML path (alias -o; default <library>-report.html in the cwd).
Suppress chatty progress output.
Absolute path of the scanned repo root. Required for {abs_path} links; enables the Lag section when it points at a git working tree.
URL pattern turning path:line labels into links. Placeholders {path}, {abs_path}, {line}.

Runs the full pipeline and emits every CoverageProfile in the corpus.
CSV and JSON go to stdout; HTML is written as a multi-page browser
into --output.
Path to sheaf.textproto.
Output format — csv, json, or html.
Output directory (required for html; ignored for csv/json).
Project repo root.

Scans two checkouts of the same project (--base = PR target, --repo
= PR head) using the same sheaf.textproto, builds a delta, and
renders a Markdown comment showing coverage gained or lost. The
rendered body always goes to stdout. With --post, it is also
delivered via the configured review adapter (noop / file / gerrit /
github).
Base repo root (PR base) — required for delta.
Path to sheaf.textproto (used for both base + head).
When --review=file, write here. Defaults to $SHEAF_REVIEW_FILE_OUT.
Post the rendered comment via the configured review adapter.
PR reference for the comment header.
Head repo root (PR head).
Override the configured review adapter — noop, file, gerrit, github.

Loads sheaf.textproto, dispatches every configured contract-anchor,
test-parser, doc-parser, rendered-reference, and implements-map
adapter, builds the cross-reference index, runs every enabled
analyzer, and prints a per-stage timing summary plus a per-finding-kind
histogram. The workhorse subcommand.
Path to sheaf.textproto (default <repo>/sheaf.textproto).
Suppress per-adapter detail in output.
Path to the project repo root.

Run the ingest → index pipeline once and then keep the corpus live
behind an MCP server. Coding agents and the scanner binary consume
the server's contract-element lookup, coverage-profile lookup,
worked-example retrieval, library_snapshot, and review_pr ops.
Override mcp_server.bind.
Path to sheaf.textproto.
Override mcp_server.port.
Project repo root.
Speak MCP over newline-delimited JSON-RPC on stdin/stdout (for Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline) instead of HTTP.

Scans the project in-process — no server — and emits the library Snapshot
JSON: the exact data the report builder consumes and the MCP server's
library_snapshot op serves. Pipe it into `scanner --from-snapshot` to render
a report offline, diff it across commits to see contract movement, or feed
it to another tool. The snapshot carries a schema_version; a snapshot whose
version does not match the reader's build is rejected.
Path to sheaf.textproto (default <repo>/sheaf.textproto).
Library to snapshot. Accepts a comma-separated list to roll several libraries into one snapshot.
Label for a multi-library snapshot (default comma-joined names). Ignored for a single library.
Write the snapshot JSON here (default stdout).
Project repo root.

Re-checks a sheaf scan's headline numbers against the snapshot it was
rendered from and (optionally) the repository on disk, BEFORE the report is
shown to anyone. It reconciles every shown figure to its numerator and
denominator, decomposes blended percentages per tier (so the denominator
effect can't hide), and flags every surface at or below the low-coverage
threshold — including, at highest priority, any surface reading exactly 0%.
It prints a human "trust ledger" and can emit a machine-readable report.

With --disk (and a --repo) it also runs the on-disk oracle: a high-precision
false-negative search over the tracked tree, and — with --check-urls —
doc-URL resolution that flags dead published links. With --json it emits a
deterministic, bounded `assertions` array of attributed claims for an agent
to adjudicate; the verdicted file feeds `sheaf verify summarize` for
per-library precision.

The honesty rail is non-negotiable: a number is called wrong only when it is
provably wrong (a reconcile mismatch, or a disk-confirmed false claim).
Anything verify cannot check is surfaced as an explicit caveat, never a
finding.
With --disk, resolve a bounded sample of published doc URLs (HTTP HEAD/GET) and flag dead links. Network side effect; default off.

One-shot — build the snapshot in-process from this sheaf.textproto (with --repo and --library) and verify it, instead of --from-snapshot. Auto-locates categorization-rules.textproto next to the config.
Run the on-disk oracle (false-negative search, doc-URL resolution, ground-truth count). Requires --repo.
Ecosystem view id (cli|fidl|proto|cpp|...). Empty uses the scanner default.
Authoritative element count (from protoc / fidlc --json / the --help tree) to cross-check the denominator against; -1 = unset. Used with --disk.
Snapshot JSON to verify (produced by `sheaf snapshot`). Provide this or --config.
Write the machine-readable verify report (including the assertions array) here.
Write the human trust ledger here (default stdout).
Library to scan for the --config one-shot path.
Flag any per-tier or pooled coverage at or below this fraction for validation.
Cap how many elements the disk oracle samples (0 = built-in default).
Repo root, for disk-oracle checks and source resolution.
Cap elements sampled into the verify.json assertions array for the attribution-precision workflow (0 = built-in default of 50).
Exit non-zero (3) when the verdict is BROKEN. For CI gating.

Print the linked build version (set via -ldflags at link time,
defaulting to 0.1.0-dev) and exit. Takes no flags and runs no
pipeline.

> docs/examples/sheaf-onboard/install.sh   # then, in Claude / Cursor / Cline:  /sheaf-onboard

>
> Any agentic CLI (Gemini, Codex) drives it via [`AGENTS.md`](docs/examples/sheaf-onboard/AGENTS.md).
From a clone, build instead of install: `go build -o sheaf ./cmd/sheaf` (same flags, `./sheaf` instead of `sheaf`).
From a clone, build instead of install: `go build -o sheaf ./cmd/sheaf` (same flags, `./sheaf` instead of `sheaf`).
- [docs/cli/sheaf.md](docs/cli/sheaf.md): overview of the `sheaf` binary.
It is a separate binary from `sheaf` so the heavy report-rendering deps stay out of the main CLI.

The `sheaf` binary is the entry point for every contract-coverage operation: scanning a repository, serving its corpus over MCP, listing gaps, dumping coverage profiles, and rendering PR-review comments.
End-to-end recipes that combine the `sheaf` and `scanner` binaries.
`sheaf` resolves config files in this order:
- User-global config (`~/.sheaf/global.textproto`) layered under project config.
- **C1.** User-global config (`~/.sheaf/global.textproto`) layered under project config: defer to v1.5 when a second adopter requests it, or design now?
Some projects may want it inside a `tools/sheaf/` subdirectory.
The hosted-API clients were stubbed out of the schema (config.proto reserves their field numbers) and `sheaf` rejects any `client:` other than `local-llama` / `noop`.

concepts.raw_text: Pin the `sheaf` binary version — numbers shift across adapter versions, and a cron that auto-updates produces unmeaningful delta charts.
section_path: Playbook: Onboard a new repo to Sheaf

concepts.raw_text: - [`sheaf coverage`](sheaf_coverage.md) — the production version of single-element drill-in.

`sheaf scan`, `sheaf gaps`, `sheaf coverage`, `sheaf report`, `sheaf snapshot`, `sheaf serve`, and `sheaf review` all run the full pipeline (config load → adapter dispatch → indexer → analyzers) before producing their output.
`sheaf coverage --format` switches between the human-readable text dump and `json`, while `sheaf coverage --config` and `sheaf coverage --repo` locate the project the same way scan does.
**Coverage.** `sheaf coverage --element` is required — it names the contract element whose profile you want.
`sheaf coverage --format` switches between the human-readable text dump and `json`, while `sheaf coverage --config` and `sheaf coverage --repo` locate the project the same way scan does.
`sheaf coverage --format` switches between the human-readable text dump and `json`, while `sheaf coverage --config` and `sheaf coverage --repo` locate the project the same way scan does.

`sheaf render` and `sheaf review-html` are pipeline-free — they render from a Snapshot JSON / `delta.json` that an earlier pipeline run already produced — as are `sheaf doctor`, `sheaf version`, and `sheaf init`.
**Doctor.** `sheaf doctor` validates the config and walks every adapter.

**Probe-time** — `sheaf doctor` and the first scan attempt to actually invoke adapters; glob patterns that match no files emit warnings; missing bundle paths emit warnings unless `cache.allow_missing_bundles = true`.
Probe-time warnings appear in `sheaf doctor` output and at the top of `sheaf scan` runs but do not abort.

|
| `name` | The name recorded in row provenance and shown by `sheaf doctor`.
`sheaf doctor` shows deprecation warnings and unknown fields prominently so they don't get lost in noise.

concepts.raw_text: `sheaf doctor --config` checks a config outside the repo root, and `sheaf doctor --repo` resolves the source map relative to a different tree.
`sheaf doctor --config` checks a config outside the repo root, and `sheaf doctor --repo` resolves the source map relative to a different tree.
`sheaf doctor --repo $REPO_ROOT` is green — every adapter reports >0 matched files and >0 extracted elements.

**Gaps.** `sheaf gaps` lists findings.
`sheaf gaps --format` emits `text`, `json`, or `csv`, and `sheaf gaps --config` selects a non-default config.
`sheaf gaps --format` emits `text`, `json`, or `csv`, and `sheaf gaps --config` selects a non-default config.

concepts.raw_text: `sheaf render --from-snapshot` names the snapshot and `sheaf render --library` overrides its label; `sheaf render --ecosystem` picks the rendering shape and `sheaf render --api-level` the target level for removed-element detection.

`sheaf render --repo-root` points at the git working tree — it enables the Lag section and the `{abs_path}` links that `sheaf render --source-url-template` can emit — while `sheaf render --commit` overrides the derived short hash and `sheaf render --header-style` chooses the masthead layout.
`sheaf render --concept-docs-href` links the concept-doc sibling report, and `sheaf render --mock-overlap` swaps in fictional Overlap data for design iteration only.

Each row links to a Sheaf report produced against a real project (the self-scan links its recipe).
- [`sheaf report`](sheaf_report.md) — bulk dump of every profile.

`sheaf scan`, `sheaf gaps`, `sheaf coverage`, `sheaf report`, `sheaf snapshot`, `sheaf serve`, and `sheaf review` all run the full pipeline (config load → adapter dispatch → indexer → analyzers) before producing their output.
**Report.** `sheaf report` bulk-dumps every coverage profile.
`sheaf report --config` and `sheaf report --repo` locate the project.
`sheaf report --format` chooses `csv`, `json`, or `html`; when it is `html`, `sheaf report --output` names the directory the multi-page report is written to.
`sheaf report --format` chooses `csv`, `json`, or `html`; when it is `html`, `sheaf report --output` names the directory the multi-page report is written to.
`sheaf report --config` and `sheaf report --repo` locate the project.

`sheaf scan`, `sheaf gaps`, `sheaf coverage`, `sheaf report`, `sheaf snapshot`, `sheaf serve`, and `sheaf review` all run the full pipeline (config load → adapter dispatch → indexer → analyzers) before producing their output.

`sheaf render` and `sheaf review-html` are pipeline-free — they render from a Snapshot JSON / `delta.json` that an earlier pipeline run already produced — as are `sheaf doctor`, `sheaf version`, and `sheaf init`.
**Review.** `sheaf review` renders a PR-coverage-delta comment.
`sheaf review` renders a Markdown comment showing what coverage was gained or lost between base and head:

`sheaf review --base <base-repo-root> --repo <head-repo-root>` renders a coverage-delta comment for a pull request: which contract elements gained or lost docs, tests, or usage between the two corpora.
It needs a base tree via `sheaf review --base`; `sheaf review --repo` is the PR head and `sheaf review --pr` is the reference printed in the comment header.
`sheaf review --config` selects the config used for both base and head.

`sheaf review --review` overrides the configured review adapter (`noop`/`file`/`gerrit`/`github`), `sheaf review --file-out` is where the `file` adapter writes, and `sheaf review --post` actually posts the comment instead of printing it.

`sheaf review --review` overrides the configured review adapter (`noop`/`file`/`gerrit`/`github`), `sheaf review --file-out` is where the `file` adapter writes, and `sheaf review --post` actually posts the comment instead of printing it.
It needs a base tree via `sheaf review --base`; `sheaf review --repo` is the PR head and `sheaf review --pr` is the reference printed in the comment header.
It needs a base tree via `sheaf review --base`; `sheaf review --repo` is the PR head and `sheaf review --pr` is the reference printed in the comment header.

`sheaf review --review` overrides the configured review adapter (`noop`/`file`/`gerrit`/`github`), `sheaf review --file-out` is where the `file` adapter writes, and `sheaf review --post` actually posts the comment instead of printing it.

`--auto` autodetects the common deterministic surfaces (Rust clap CLIs, protobuf / gRPC, FIDL, and C++ headers), runs the scan, and writes a self-contained report next to the `sheaf.textproto` it generated, so a second run is just `sheaf scan`.

`sheaf scan --manifest <file>` reads a `MonorepoManifest` textproto and runs a scan + render for every entry, producing one report per module plus an `index.html` linking them, all in-process, no `sheaf serve` per module.
`sheaf scan` and iterate
- [`sheaf scan`](sheaf_scan.md) — the production scan.
- [`sheaf scan`](sheaf_scan.md) — the consumer of the generated YAML via the cobra adapter.

`sheaf scan`, `sheaf gaps`, `sheaf coverage`, `sheaf report`, `sheaf snapshot`, `sheaf serve`, and `sheaf review` all run the full pipeline (config load → adapter dispatch → indexer → analyzers) before producing their output.
**Scan.** A bare `sheaf scan` runs against `<repo>/sheaf.textproto`.
Probe-time warnings appear in `sheaf doctor` output and at the top of `sheaf scan` runs but do not abort.
**Severity meanings.** `INFO` is reported but never blocks; `WARNING` increments the warning count (`sheaf scan` exit code 1 if any); `ERROR` increments the error count (exit code 2).
The unit of a Sheaf scan is one project = one `sheaf.textproto`.

Q6. Is this a monorepo, and if so what's the unit of scan?

concepts.raw_text: The one fail gate is `sheaf scan --manifest <file> --fail-on-error`, which returns a non-zero exit code if any manifest entry failed to scan/render (it does not fail on findings).

concepts.raw_text: Point `sheaf scan --config` at a config that lives elsewhere, set the project root with `sheaf scan --repo`, and pass `sheaf scan --quiet` to drop the per-adapter sample blocks when you only want the corpus summary.

concepts.raw_text: `sheaf scan --manifest <file>` reads a `MonorepoManifest` textproto and runs a scan + render for every entry, producing one report per module plus an `index.html` linking them, all in-process, no `sheaf serve` per module.
Connect to a running `sheaf serve` MCP server, pick a library, and write a self-contained HTML fragmentation report.
- [`sheaf serve`](sheaf_serve.md) — the MCP server the scanner connects to.

`sheaf scan`, `sheaf gaps`, `sheaf coverage`, `sheaf report`, `sheaf snapshot`, `sheaf serve`, and `sheaf review` all run the full pipeline (config load → adapter dispatch → indexer → analyzers) before producing their output.
**Serve.** `sheaf serve` starts the MCP server.

| Binary | What it does | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| [`scanner`](reference/scanner.md) | Talk to a running `sheaf serve` and write a self-contained HTML fragmentation report.
`sheaf serve` exposes the corpus over JSON-RPC.
The preferred path is fully in-process — no `sheaf serve`, no `scanner` — using `sheaf snapshot` to persist the Snapshot JSON and `sheaf render --from-snapshot` to render it:
section_path: Render a report after the server is gone

concepts.raw_text: The older two-binary route — standing up `sheaf serve` and persisting with `scanner --snapshot-out` — still works, but `scanner --snapshot-out` is **deprecated**; prefer `sheaf snapshot` above.
section_path: Render a report after the server is gone

concepts.raw_text: - Hot-reload on config change during a running `sheaf serve`.

| backend `DefaultModel` | `internal/llm/anthropic/anthropic.go:72` |
| `SHEAF_LOG_FORMAT` | Log encoding for `sheaf serve`: `json` → JSON logs; any other value → text.
- **C5.** Hot reload on config change during `sheaf serve`: nice to have, v2.

| Stage | Doc |
|---|---|
| Start the server          | [`sheaf serve`](../cli/reference/sheaf_serve.md) |
| List libraries            | [`list_libraries`](api.md#list_libraries) |
| Bulk-pull one library     | [`library_snapshot`](api.md#library_snapshot) |
| Drill into one element    | [`query_contract`](api.md#query_contract), [`coverage`](api.md#coverage) |
| Find by description       | [`find_examples`](api.md#find_examples) |
| Verify an invocation      | [`verify_invocation`](api.md#verify_invocation) |
| Worklist of gaps          | [`find_coverage_gaps`](api.md#find_coverage_gaps) |
| PR review                 | [`review_pr`](api.md#review_pr) |
| Health probe              | `GET /healthz` |

The MCP server exposed by [`sheaf serve`](../cli/reference/sheaf_serve.md) is a JSON-RPC 2.0 endpoint that serves Sheaf's in-memory corpus to coding agents, the [`scanner`](../cli/reference/scanner.md) binary, and any other client — over **HTTP** or, for desktop MCP clients that spawn the server as a subprocess, over **stdio**.
`sheaf serve` speaks the same JSON-RPC operations over two transports.
This op is **opt-in**: the server only exposes it after the host has called `WithReview` (which the CLI does when `sheaf serve` parses `review { ...
- [`sheaf serve`](../cli/reference/sheaf_serve.md) — how to start the server.
`sheaf serve --bind` sets the listen address (default `127.0.0.1`) and `sheaf serve --config` selects the config whose corpus is served.

Example: `sheaf serve --bind` / `--port` override
  `mcp_server.bind` / `mcp_server.port` (`internal/cli/extra.go:491`).
`sheaf serve --bind` sets the listen address (default `127.0.0.1`) and `sheaf serve --config` selects the config whose corpus is served.

concepts.raw_text: If the maintainer's team uses Claude / Cursor day-to-day, expose the scan via `sheaf serve --port 7700` and document the MCP endpoint.
section_path: Playbook: Onboard a new repo to Sheaf

concepts.raw_text: Start it with `sheaf serve --repo .` and point your agent at `http://127.0.0.1:7700`; queries include contract elements, coverage profiles, and worked examples per element.

`sheaf serve --stdio` switches from the HTTP listener to newline-delimited JSON-RPC on stdin/stdout, the transport desktop MCP clients (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline) use when they spawn the server as a subprocess.
- [`sheaf snapshot`](sheaf_snapshot.md) — the preferred, in-process way to persist a Snapshot JSON (replaces `scanner --snapshot-out`).

`sheaf scan`, `sheaf gaps`, `sheaf coverage`, `sheaf report`, `sheaf snapshot`, `sheaf serve`, and `sheaf review` all run the full pipeline (config load → adapter dispatch → indexer → analyzers) before producing their output.
**Snapshot.** `sheaf snapshot` emits a library's Snapshot JSON for the server-free render path.
The preferred path is fully in-process — no `sheaf serve`, no `scanner` — using `sheaf snapshot` to persist the Snapshot JSON and `sheaf render --from-snapshot` to render it:
section_path: Render a report after the server is gone

concepts.raw_text: The older two-binary route — standing up `sheaf serve` and persisting with `scanner --snapshot-out` — still works, but `scanner --snapshot-out` is **deprecated**; prefer `sheaf snapshot` above.
section_path: Render a report after the server is gone

concepts.raw_text: Generate the same payload offline — no server — with [`sheaf snapshot`](../cli/reference/sheaf_snapshot.md), then re-render it via `scanner --from-snapshot`.
(The `scanner` binary's own `--snapshot-out` flag persists this verbatim too, but is deprecated in favor of `sheaf snapshot`.)
This is the in-process path — `sheaf snapshot` emits the library's Snapshot JSON and `sheaf render` turns it into a self-contained HTML file, no server.
section_path: Playbook: Onboard a new repo to Sheaf
Stage 6: scan, validate by hand, then render

concepts.raw_text: `sheaf snapshot --library` names the library (a comma-separated list rolls several into one snapshot, labelled by `sheaf snapshot --library-label`), `sheaf snapshot --out` is the destination file, and `sheaf snapshot --config` / `sheaf snapshot --repo` locate the project.

`sheaf snapshot --library` names the library (a comma-separated list rolls several into one snapshot, labelled by `sheaf snapshot --library-label`), `sheaf snapshot --out` is the destination file, and `sheaf snapshot --config` / `sheaf snapshot --repo` locate the project.

`sheaf snapshot --library` names the library (a comma-separated list rolls several into one snapshot, labelled by `sheaf snapshot --library-label`), `sheaf snapshot --out` is the destination file, and `sheaf snapshot --config` / `sheaf snapshot --repo` locate the project.
`--snapshot-out <path>` persists the snapshot for offline replay, but is **deprecated** — prefer `sheaf snapshot --out`, which produces the same JSON in-process without a server.
Prefer `sheaf snapshot --out` (in-process, no server).

`sheaf snapshot --library` names the library (a comma-separated list rolls several into one snapshot, labelled by `sheaf snapshot --library-label`), `sheaf snapshot --out` is the destination file, and `sheaf snapshot --config` / `sheaf snapshot --repo` locate the project.

> **Onboard your repo with the `sheaf-onboard` skill (recommended).** Your agent does the reconnaissance and config, runs the scan, then runs `sheaf verify` to check every headline number against disk *before* it shows you the report, so your spot-check is a confirmation, not a discovery.
**Verify.** `sheaf verify` adversarially re-checks a scan's headline numbers before the report is shown.
The verdicted assertions feed `sheaf verify summarize` for per-library precision.

`sheaf verify --disk` runs the on-disk oracle — `sheaf verify --check-urls` adds dead-link resolution, `sheaf verify --max-disk-elements` caps its sample, and `sheaf verify --expected-elements` cross-checks the element count against an authoritative parser — while `sheaf verify --sample-assertions` bounds the attribution `assertions` array that `sheaf verify --json` writes for the precision workflow.

It reads a snapshot via `sheaf verify --from-snapshot` and reconciles it — or, as a one-shot, `sheaf verify --config` builds the snapshot in-process (with `sheaf verify --library`) and verifies it in a single step.

`sheaf verify --disk` runs the on-disk oracle — `sheaf verify --check-urls` adds dead-link resolution, `sheaf verify --max-disk-elements` caps its sample, and `sheaf verify --expected-elements` cross-checks the element count against an authoritative parser — while `sheaf verify --sample-assertions` bounds the attribution `assertions` array that `sheaf verify --json` writes for the precision workflow.
`sheaf verify --repo` points at the source tree, `sheaf verify --ecosystem` selects the view shape, and `sheaf verify --low-coverage-threshold` sets the suspicion line.

`sheaf verify --ledger` writes the human trust ledger and `sheaf verify --strict` exits non-zero when the verdict is broken.

`sheaf render` and `sheaf review-html` are pipeline-free — they render from a Snapshot JSON / `delta.json` that an earlier pipeline run already produced — as are `sheaf doctor`, `sheaf version`, and `sheaf init`.
